title: James J. Andrews and William H. Campbell Congressional Gold Medal Act
short_title: James J. Andrews and William H. Campbell Congressional Gold Medal Act
plain_summary: This bill creates a special Congressional Gold Medal to honor two brave civilians from the Civil War named James J. Andrews and William H. Campbell. They were part of a famous military mission called the Great Locomotive Chase where they stole a train to help the Union Army. The medal will be put on display at the Smithsonian Museum for everyone to see. The government can also make bronze copies of the medal to sell to people who want to buy them.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Financial Services, and in addition to the Committee on House Administration, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the James J. Andrews and William H. Campbell Congressional Gold Medal Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress has learned these facts about two important Civil War heroes. James J. Andrews was born in eighteen twenty nine in what is now West Virginia. He later moved to Kentucky. During the Civil War, Andrews worked as a spy for the Union Army and led a group called Andrews' Raiders. William Hunter Campbell was born in Ohio on September ninth, eighteen thirty nine. In eighteen sixty two, he was visiting friends with the Second Ohio Infantry in Kentucky when he was asked to join a dangerous mission. On March twenty fifth, eighteen sixty two, James J. Andrews made a plan to cut off an important Confederate railroad supply line. The railroad ran from Marietta, Georgia, to Chattanooga, Tennessee. Andrews wanted to help the Union Army attack and take over the city. He presented his plan to General Buell and General O.M. Mitchell, who were commanding the Union Army in North Alabama. They approved the plan. Andrews gathered twenty two Union soldiers from three Ohio army units. The plan was to work in small groups from northern Alabama. They would dress in regular clothes and travel to Marietta, Georgia. There they would steal a train and drive it toward Chattanooga while cutting telegraph lines, tearing up railroad tracks, and burning bridges if possible. This would stop Confederate troops and supplies from reaching Chattanooga. Taking Chattanooga early in the war would cut off important supplies and food from reaching Virginia and the Confederate Army there. On April twelfth, eighteen sixty two, twenty four Union Army volunteers led by civilian scout James J. Andrews stole a Confederate train called the General near Big Shanty, Georgia which is now called Kennesaw. They drove the train north toward Chattanooga, Tennessee, and damaged the important Western and Atlantic Railroad line as they went. When they ran out of fuel, Andrews and his men left the train and scattered into the woods before Confederate troops captured them. All the captured men were put on trial and found guilty of unlawful fighting and spying. Soon after, Andrews and seven of the Raiders were killed by hanging. The others were held as prisoners of war. Andrews' body was buried after he was executed. On October sixteenth, eighteen eighty seven, his remains were moved to their final resting place at Chattanooga National Cemetery. William H. Campbell was hanged with six of the military men on June eighteenth, eighteen sixty two. In April eighteen sixty six, these men were moved and buried in Chattanooga National Cemetery. On March twenty fifth, eighteen sixty three, six members of Andrews' Raiders received the first Medals of Honor in our nation's history. Eventually, twenty one of the twenty four members of Andrews' Raiders would receive the Medal of Honor for their actions that day. On July third, twenty twenty four, President Biden awarded the most recent Medals of Honor to members of Andrews' Raiders. He said their heroic deeds went unrecognized for over a century, but time did not erase their courage. Chattanooga, Tennessee, is recognized as the birthplace of the Medal of Honor. In nineteen fifty six, Walt Disney made a movie about Andrews' Raiders called The Great Locomotive Chase, starring Fess Parker as Andrews. Buster Keaton's nineteen twenty seven movie The General was also loosely based on this incident. Today, the stolen train called the General is on display at The Southern Museum in Kennesaw, Georgia. The train called Texas that chased after it is on display at the Atlanta History Center in Atlanta, Georgia. The two civilians in Andrews' Raiders, James J. Andrews and William Hunter Campbell, showed extraordinary courage and complete devotion to their country as they tried to change the course of the Civil War.

sections.title: Section 3  Congressional Gold Medal
sections.text: The Speaker of the House of Representatives and the President pro tempore (temporary president) of the Senate shall arrange for the presentation of a single gold medal on behalf of Congress. This medal will honor both James J. Andrews and William H. Campbell together. It recognizes their extraordinary courage and complete devotion to the country during the Civil War as the only civilian members of Andrews' Raiders, who carried out a daring military mission that became known as the Great Locomotive Chase. The Secretary of the Treasury will create and make a gold medal with appropriate symbols, designs, and words as determined by the Secretary. After the gold medal is awarded, it shall be given to the Smithsonian Institution where it will be available for display as appropriate and for research purposes. Congress believes that the Smithsonian Institution should make the gold medal available for display, especially at the Charles H. Coolidge National Medal of Honor Museum, and for loan as appropriate so that the medal may be displayed in other places.

sections.title: Section 4  Duplicate Medals
sections.text: The Secretary may create and sell copies of the gold medal made of bronze for a price that covers the cost of making them, including labor, materials, the metal molds used to make them, use of machinery, and overhead expenses.

sections.title: Section 5  Status of Medals
sections.text: Medals made under this law are national medals for purposes of chapter fifty one of title thirty one, United States Code. For purposes of sections five thousand one hundred thirty four and five thousand one hundred thirty six of title thirty one, United States Code, all medals made under this law shall be considered to be collectible coin items.

sections.title: Section 6  Authority to Use Fund Amounts and Proceeds of Sale
sections.text: The United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund is authorized to be charged the amounts needed to pay for the costs of the medals made under this law. Money received from selling the duplicate bronze medals allowed under section four shall be put into the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund.
